message.content:
Here is an exhaustive list of every **Information Gathering (WSTG‑INFO)** and **Configuration and Deployment Management Testing (WSTG‑CONF)** test from the OWASP Web Security Testing Guide **v4.2/stable**, with the exact ID, name, what it verifies, and the canonical OWASP URL.[2][3][6]

> Note: OWASP’s canonical IDs follow the pattern `WSTG-<category>-<number>`, where `INFO` = Information Gathering and `CONF` = Configuration and Deployment Management Testing.[1][2]  
> The per‑chapter URLs are under the “stable” tree for the current guide.[6]

---

## WSTG‑INFO – Information Gathering

### WSTG‑INFO‑01 – Conduct Search Engine Discovery and Reconnaissance for Information Leakage

- **What it verifies:**  
  That public search engines and cached/archived content do not expose **sensitive information** about the application, its infrastructure, users, or data (for example via Google dorks, cached pages, code snippets, credentials, internal hosts, or error messages).[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/01-Conduct_Search_Engine_Discovery_Reconnaissance_for_Information_Leakage`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑02 – Fingerprint Web Server

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the underlying **web server software and version** cannot be reliably identified through banners, headers, error messages, default files, or protocol behavior, or that any such identification does not introduce undue risk; this test enumerates the server type, version, modules, and OS where possible.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/02-Fingerprint_Web_Server`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑03 – Review Webserver Metafiles for Information Leakage

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **metafiles** such as `robots.txt`, `sitemap.xml`, `crossdomain.xml`, `security.txt`, and similar discovery aids do not disclose sensitive paths, admin areas, temporary resources, backup locations, or other information that can expand the attack surface.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/03-Review_Webserver_Metafiles_for_Information_Leakage`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑04 – Enumerate Applications on Webserver

- **What it verifies:**  
  That there are no **unnecessary, forgotten, or undocumented applications** or virtual hosts exposed on the same web server, by enumerating sites, directories, vhosts, and services to map all reachable applications and technologies.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/04-Enumerate_Applications_on_Webserver`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑05 – Review Web Page Content for Information Leakage

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the HTML and client‑side assets do not leak sensitive information; the test reviews **page content, comments, metadata, JavaScript, source maps, and downloadable files** for credentials, API keys, internal IPs/hostnames, debug info, framework versions, and similar leaks.[3][6][7]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/05-Review_Web_Page_Content_for_Information_Leakage`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑06 – Identify Application Entry Points

- **What it verifies:**  
  That all **input and interaction points** (URLs, forms, query parameters, headers, JSON/XML fields, file uploads, APIs, WebSocket endpoints, etc.) are identified and mapped so subsequent security tests cover the full attack surface.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/06-Identify_Application_Entry_Points`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑07 – Map Execution Paths Through Application

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **execution flows and navigation paths** through the application are understood: how users move between pages and functions, pre‑conditions to access features, workflows (e.g., registration, checkout), and how state changes, in order to identify complex or chained attack paths.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/07-Map_Execution_Paths_Through_Application`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑08 – Fingerprint Web Application Framework

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the **web application framework** (e.g., Django, Laravel, Spring, Rails) and its version are either not trivially discoverable, or if discoverable, are updated and hardened; this test fingerprints framework‑specific headers, URLs, resources, error pages, and response patterns.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/08-Fingerprint_Web_Application_Framework`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑09 – Fingerprint Web Application

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the **custom application itself** is not leaking unnecessary identifying information (product name, version, build IDs, component names) through titles, headers, cookies, comments, JavaScript, or error messages; the test aims to characterize the application stack and components.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/09-Fingerprint_Web_Application`

---

### WSTG‑INFO‑10 – Map Application Architecture

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the **overall architecture** of the application and its environment can be understood from externally observable behavior without exposing unnecessary details; the test infers tiers, load balancers, CDN, microservices, third‑party integrations, and supporting services, to understand where security controls must apply.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/01-Information_Gathering/10-Map_Application_Architecture`

---

## WSTG‑CONF – Configuration and Deployment Management Testing

### WSTG‑CONF‑01 – Test Network Infrastructure Configuration

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **network‑level configuration** around the web application is secure: firewalls, load balancers, reverse proxies, routing, exposed ports and services, and segregation between tiers, ensuring only required services/ports are reachable and network devices are not misconfigured.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/01-Test_Network_Infrastructure_Configuration`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑02 – Test Application Platform Configuration

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the **application platform / middleware** (web server, app server, language runtime, framework runtime) is securely configured: removal of default content, unused modules, sample apps and admin consoles; secure TLS/cipher settings; appropriate timeouts and logging; and no verbose error or debug configurations in production.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/02-Test_Application_Platform_Configuration`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑03 – Test File Extensions Handling for Sensitive Information

- **What it verifies:**  
  That the server’s **file extension and MIME‑type handling** does not inadvertently expose source code, configuration, or sensitive data (e.g., downloading `.bak`, `.old`, `.config`, `.inc`, or unparsed templates), and that content negotiation or mis‑mapping cannot be abused to retrieve non‑public content.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/03-Test_File_Extensions_Handling_for_Sensitive_Information`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑04 – Review Old Backup and Unreferenced Files for Sensitive Information

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **backup, temporary, and unreferenced files** (e.g., `*.bak`, `*.old`, `.zip`, `.tar`, editor backup files, old deployments) are not left accessible under the web root and do not contain credentials, configurations, source code, database dumps, or other sensitive content.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/04-Review_Old_Backup_and_Unreferenced_Files_for_Sensitive_Information`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑05 – Enumerate Infrastructure and Application Admin Interfaces

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **administrative interfaces** (for infrastructure devices, databases, message brokers, application consoles, framework backends, etc.) are not unnecessarily exposed to the internet or untrusted networks, and that any exposed admin interfaces are properly authenticated, authorized, and restricted.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/05-Enumerate_Infrastructure_and_Application_Admin_Interfaces`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑06 – Test HTTP Methods

- **What it verifies:**  
  That only **necessary HTTP methods** are enabled and correctly enforced; the test checks for dangerous or misconfigured methods such as `PUT`, `DELETE`, `TRACE`, `TRACK`, `CONNECT`, `OPTIONS`, WebDAV methods, and whether method overrides can bypass access controls.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/06-Test_HTTP_Methods`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑07 – Test HTTP Strict Transport Security

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)** is correctly configured on HTTPS endpoints: presence of `Strict-Transport-Security` header, appropriate `max-age`, use of `includeSubDomains` and optionally `preload`, and that it effectively enforces HTTPS and mitigates SSL stripping and protocol downgrades.[3][4][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/07-Test_HTTP_Strict_Transport_Security`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑08 – Test RIA Cross Domain Policy

- **What it verifies:**  
  That legacy **Rich Internet Application cross‑domain policy files** (such as `crossdomain.xml` for Flash or `clientaccesspolicy.xml` for Silverlight) are either absent where not needed or are restricted to trusted domains and do not allow overly broad cross‑domain requests that could expose internal services or data.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/08-Test_RIA_Cross_Domain_Policy`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑09 – Test File Permission

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **file system permissions** on web‑accessible and supporting directories are correctly set: web server user has the minimum required rights; sensitive files and directories (config, logs, keys, scripts) are not world‑readable or writable; and directory traversal cannot be used to access unauthorized files.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/09-Test_File_Permission`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑10 – Test for Subdomain Takeover

- **What it verifies:**  
  That there are no **dangling DNS records** (e.g., CNAMEs or other records pointing to de‑provisioned cloud resources, SaaS tenants, or hosting providers) that would allow an attacker to register the underlying resource and take over the subdomain to host arbitrary content or impersonate the application.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/10-Test_for_Subdomain_Takeover`

---

### WSTG‑CONF‑11 – Test Cloud Storage

- **What it verifies:**  
  That **cloud object storage and similar services** (e.g., S3 buckets, Azure Blob/Files, GCP Storage) used by the application are securely configured: correct access control (no public read/write where not intended), proper authentication and encryption, no listing of private objects, and no exposure of backups or internal data through misconfigured buckets or shares.[3][6]

- **Canonical OWASP URL:**  
  `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/stable/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/02-Configuration_and_Deployment_Management_Testing/11-Test_Cloud_Storage`
